plan.title: Association between self-reported stroke history and metabolic syndrome components in US adults (NHANES 2011-2018)
plan.question: Is self-reported stroke history in US adults associated with metabolic syndrome components, pooling NHANES 2011-2018?

covariates.name: smoke_ever
covariates.label: Smoked at least 100 cigarettes in lifetime

steps.description: Merge NHANES cycles G-J and restrict to adults with complete metabolic syndrome measures
Apply MEC examination survey design weights (WTMEC2YR) with PSU and strata
Calculate descriptive statistics for metabolic syndrome components by stroke status
Fit survey-weighted logistic regression models for each metabolic syndrome component, adjusting for age, sex, race, and smoking

causal_claims: Self-reported stroke history may be associated with metabolic syndrome components in cross-sectional analysis.
Observed associations may be confounded by age, smoking, and other cardiovascular risk factors.
Temporal directionality between metabolic syndrome and stroke cannot be established due to cross-sectional design.
plan.rationale: NHANES contains self-reported stroke (MCQ160F) and objective measures for key metabolic syndrome components (BMI, waist circumference, blood pressure, fasting glucose, insulin) across cycles G-J (2011-2018). Fasting subsample restrictions apply for glucose and insulin measures, which may reduce effective sample size. Cross-sectional design limits causal inference.
plan.guardrail_notes: Stroke outcome is self-reported without timing or severity detail. Cross-sectional design precludes establishing temporal sequence. Fasting subsample required for glucose and insulin may introduce selection bias. MEC examination weights apply only to those examined in the MEC; missing data on metabolic components may bias estimates if not missing at random.

plan.title: Self-Reported Stroke History and Metabolic Syndrome Components: NHANES 2011-2018
plan.question: Is self-reported stroke history in US adults associated with metabolic syndrome components, pooling NHANES 2011-2018?

outcome.name: stroke_history
outcome.label: Self-reported stroke history
outcome.ref_notes: Binary indicator (1=yes, 0=no); may be proxy-reported; validation studies suggest moderate accuracy

exposures.name: waist_circumference
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - waist circumference
exposures.ref_notes: Continuous variable; can dichotomize at sex-specific cutpoints (men: ≥102 cm, women: ≥88 cm) for metabolic syndrome definition

exposures.name: systolic_bp
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - systolic BP
exposures.transform: average of BPXSY1-BPXSY4; need to handle missing readings
exposures.ref_notes: Average of up to 4 readings; dichotomize at ≥130 mmHg or on BP meds for metabolic syndrome

exposures.name: diastolic_bp
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - diastolic BP
exposures.transform: average of BPXDI1-BPXDI4; need to handle missing readings
exposures.ref_notes: Average of up to 4 readings; dichotomize at ≥85 mmHg or on BP meds for metabolic syndrome

exposures.name: fasting_glucose
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - fasting glucose
exposures.ref_notes: Dichotomize at ≥100 mg/dL or on diabetes meds for metabolic syndrome

exposures.name: triglycerides
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - triglycerides
exposures.ref_notes: Fasting required; dichotomize at ≥150 mg/dL or on meds for metabolic syndrome

exposures.name: hdl_cholesterol
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - HDL cholesterol
exposures.ref_notes: Sex-specific cutpoints for metabolic syndrome: men <40 mg/dL, women <50 mg/dL

exposures.name: metabolic_syndrome_count
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - composite count
exposures.transform: Construct from component variables; define metabolic syndrome as ≥3 components
exposures.ref_notes: Constructed variable: count of metabolic syndrome components present (0-5)

exposures.name: metabolic_syndrome
exposures.label: Metabolic syndrome - binary
exposures.transform: Construct from component count; threshold at ≥3 components per ATP III criteria
exposures.ref_notes: Binary indicator: 1 if ≥3 metabolic syndrome components present, 0 otherwise

covariates.name: age
covariates.label: Age
covariates.ref_notes: RIDAGEYR - age in years at screening

covariates.name: smoking_status
covariates.label: Smoking status
covariates.ref_notes: SMQ040 - current smoker? SMQ020 - smoked at least 100 cigarettes

covariates.name: physical_activity
covariates.label: Physical activity
covariates.ref_notes: PAD680 - total minutes of moderate work activity; need to construct MET-hours

covariates.name: alcohol_use
covariates.label: Alcohol use
covariates.ref_notes: ALQ101 - had at least 12 drinks in lifetime; ALQ110 - had at least 12 drinks in past year

covariates.name: diabetes_history
covariates.label: History of diabetes
covariates.ref_notes: DIQ010 - told by doctor you have diabetes

covariates.name: cvd_history
covariates.label: History of CVD (excluding stroke)
covariates.ref_notes: MCQ160 - coronary heart disease; MCQ160C - heart attack; need to exclude stroke from composite

covariates.name: bmi
covariates.label: BMI
covariates.ref_notes: BMXBMI - BMI (kg/m^2) from measured weight and height

covariates.name: cholesterol_meds
covariates.label: Cholesterol medication use
covariates.ref_notes: RXQ180 - prescription cholesterol medication; need to map from drug classes in prescription database

covariates.name: bp_meds
covariates.label: Blood pressure medication use
covariates.ref_notes: RXQ192 - prescription blood pressure medication; need to map from drug classes

covariates.name: diabetes_meds
covariates.label: Diabetes medication use
covariates.ref_notes: RXQ193 - prescription diabetes medication; need to map from drug classes

steps.description: Merge NHANES 2011-2018 cycles (4 cycles) and restrict to adults age 20+ with complete stroke history data
Restrict to fasting subsample (participants with fasting laboratory values) for metabolic syndrome component analysis
Apply NHANES survey design with stratification, clustering, and 4-year weights (WTMEC2YR/4)

Construct metabolic syndrome components: dichotomize each at ATP III cutpoints (waist: men ≥102 cm, women ≥88 cm; BP: SBP ≥130 or DBP ≥85 or BP meds; glucose: ≥100 or diabetes meds; triglycerides: ≥150 or meds; HDL: men <40, women <50 or meds)
Perform descriptive statistics: prevalence of stroke history, distribution of metabolic syndrome components, sample characteristics

Conduct primary analysis: logistic regression of stroke history on individual metabolic syndrome components (adjusted for demographics, lifestyle factors, BMI, diabetes history, CVD history, medication use)
Conduct secondary analysis: logistic regression of stroke history on metabolic syndrome count (continuous, 0-5) and binary metabolic syndrome (≥3 components)

Conduct sensitivity analyses: (a) exclude participants with CVD history, (b) adjust for additional covariates, (c) use alternative metabolic syndrome definitions, (d) multiple imputation for missing covariate data
Report results with appropriate survey design adjustments, confidence intervals, and population-weighted estimates

causal_claims: Self-reported stroke history is cross-sectionally associated with metabolic syndrome components in US adults.
Association may be modified by individual metabolic syndrome components.
Findings suggest but do not prove temporal relationship between stroke and metabolic dysfunction.
plan.rationale: Feasible: NHANES contains self-reported stroke history (MCQ220) and all metabolic syndrome components (waist circumference, blood pressure, fasting glucose, triglycerides, HDL) across cycles 2011-2018. Analysis requires pooling 4 two-year cycles (2011-2012, 2013-2014, 2015-2016, 2017-2018). Variables identified: stroke history as primary outcome; metabolic syndrome components as exposures. Fasting subsample required for laboratory components (glucose, triglycerides, HDL) - this constrains analysis sample. Survey design variables (SDMVPSU, SDMVSTRA, WTMEC2YR) available for all cycles. Covariates include demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, education), lifestyle factors (smoking, physical activity, alcohol), cardiovascular risk factors (BMI, diabetes history, CVD history, medication use). Cross-sectional design limits causal inference but allows assessment of associations. Findings likely generalizable to US non-institutionalized adult population, though fasting subsample may introduce selection bias.
plan.guardrail_notes: Analysis restricted to fasting subsample for metabolic syndrome components; may reduce sample size and statistical power. Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference - reverse causation possible (stroke may lead to metabolic changes). Self-reported stroke history may be subject to recall bias and misclassification. Metabolic syndrome definition requires harmonization across NHANES cycles - verify comparability of laboratory methods and cutpoints. Survey design requires appropriate weighting and variance estimation. Missing data on fasting subsample may not be missing completely at random.

guardrail_findings.message: Causal/overclaiming word ''prove'' in: 'Findings suggest but do not prove temporal relationship between stroke and metabolic dysfunction.'
guardrail_findings.remediation: Observational NHANES data cannot support causal claims. Use 'is associated with', 'suggests', or 'may'.

guardrail_findings.remediation: Fasting labs require WTSAF2YR, not WTMEC2YR.

plan.title: Feasibility assessment: stroke_02
plan.question: Does laparoscopic bariatric surgery reduce 5-year stroke recurrence?

steps.description: Deterministic routing assessment: NHANES has no procedure/surgery data

plan.rationale: NHANES has no procedure/surgery data
plan.guardrail_notes: Deterministic router: NHANES - NHANES has no procedure/surgery data

plan.title: Infeasible: Bariatric surgery and stroke recurrence
plan.question: Does laparoscopic bariatric surgery reduce 5-year stroke recurrence?
plan.rationale: This question is infeasible for NHANES analysis for three fundamental reasons: (1) NHANES is primarily cross-sectional and does not have longitudinal follow-up for stroke recurrence over 5 years. While NHANES has linked mortality data through NCHS, it does not capture non-fatal stroke recurrence events. (2) NHANES does not contain detailed bariatric surgery data - medical questionnaires (MCQ) may have self-reported weight loss surgery history, but lack surgical procedure details (laparoscopic vs open), procedure dates, and pre/post-surgical temporal markers needed for treatment effect analysis. (3) To assess whether surgery 'reduces' stroke recurrence requires longitudinal follow-up starting from the time of surgery with repeated outcome assessment, which NHANES study design cannot provide. Alternative data sources such as bariatric surgery registries, claims databases (e.g., Medicare, private insurance claims), or prospective cohort studies with detailed surgical and stroke outcome data would be needed to address this question.
plan.guardrail_notes: None - question is fundamentally infeasible for NHANES

guardrail_findings.remediation: NHANES requires SDMVSTRA as the strata variable.

plan.title: Stroke prevalence by sarcopenia status in adults 60+, NHANES 2013-2018
plan.question: Stroke prevalence by sarcopenia status, adults 60+, NHANES 2013-2018

exposures.name: sarcopenia_status
exposures.label: Sarcopenia classification (requires DXA, grip strength, gait speed - not in registry)
exposures.transform: Requires combination of muscle mass (DXA), grip strength, and gait speed - none available in registry
exposures.ref_notes: Sarcopenia cannot be operationalized with provided registry variables. Analysis requires: DXA total body composition (DXX) for appendicular lean mass, grip strength (MCQ230/MCXQ), and gait speed (PAQ) - all absent from current registry.

covariates.name: age
covariates.label: Age in years
covariates.transform: Filter to >=60, include as continuous covariate

steps.description: Merge NHANES 2013-2018 cycles (G, H, I, J) and apply appropriate interview and exam weights
outputs: Merged dataset with 4 cycles of data

steps.description: Apply inclusion criteria: adults aged 60+ years with complete stroke and covariate data

Define sarcopenia exposure - NOTE: Not feasible with current registry. Requires: (a) appendicular lean mass from DXA, (b) grip strength, (c) gait speed. Must expand registry to include DXX, MCX, and PAQ modules.
Apply complex survey design with SDMVPSU, SDMVSTRA, and MEC weight
Calculate stroke prevalence by sarcopenia status using svy procedures
Assess cross-sectional association using survey-weighted logistic regression
outputs: Adjusted odds ratios for stroke by sarcopenia status

causal_claims: Cross-sectional association between sarcopenia and stroke prevalence may be observed.
Temporal sequence cannot be established due to NHANES cross-sectional design.
Sarcopenia likely precedes stroke in pathophysiology, but self-reported stroke timing limits causal inference.
plan.rationale: Analysis is technically feasible per routing decision, BUT critical limitation exists: sarcopenia cannot be operationalized with the provided registry variables. Sarcopenia definition per EWGSOP2 requires: low muscle strength (grip strength), low muscle mass (DXA appendicular lean mass), and low physical performance (gait speed). None of these components exist in the current registry. Stroke outcome (MCQ160F) is available. Covariates (age, sex, race, BMI) are available. The analysis requires expanding the variable registry to include DXA body composition (DXX), muscle strength (MCXQ), and physical activity (PAQ) modules.
plan.guardrail_notes: CRITICAL: Sarcopenia exposure cannot be constructed from provided registry. Must add DXA, grip strength, and gait speed variables. Stroke is self-reported with unknown timing and severity; prevalence likely underestimated. Cross-sectional design prohibits causal claims about sarcopenia causing stroke or stroke causing sarcopenia. Survey weights must be applied for all prevalence estimates.

guardrail_findings.remediation: Acceptable for scaffolding; resolve before any reported result.

plan.title: Stroke prevalence by sarcopenia status in older adults
plan.question: Stroke prevalence by sarcopenia status, adults 60+, NHANES 2013-2018

exposures.name: sarcopenia_status
exposures.label: Derived sarcopenia classification
exposures.transform: Derived from DXA lean mass and grip strength per EWGSOP2 criteria

exposures.name: alm_bmi
exposures.label: Appendicular lean mass adjusted for BMI

steps.description: Identify stroke cases from MCQ160F (doctor told you had stroke)
Calculate appendicular lean mass (ALM) from DXA: DXXARML + DXXLEG
Calculate ALM/BMI ratio for low muscle strength definition
Identify grip strength from PAQ710 (maximum hand grip)
Derive sarcopenia status per EWGSOP2: probable sarcopenia (low grip strength), confirmed sarcopenia (low grip + low ALM/BMI), severe sarcopenia (low grip + low ALM/BMI + low gait speed if available)
Apply inclusion criteria: age >= 60 years, have DXA and grip strength data, have stroke outcome
Adjust survey weights: WTMEC2YR / 3 (for 3 cycles)
Apply survey design with SDMVPSU, SDMVSTRA, adjusted weights
Calculate stroke prevalence by sarcopenia status with 95% CIs using survey procedures
Logistic regression of stroke on sarcopenia adjusting for covariates (age, sex, race/ethnicity, hypertension, diabetes, CHD, smoking, physical activity)

causal_claims: Sarcopenia may be associated with higher stroke prevalence in cross-sectional analysis.
The association between sarcopenia and stroke may be partially explained by shared cardiovascular risk factors.
Directionality cannot be established from cross-sectional data - stroke may lead to muscle loss or sarcopenia may increase stroke risk.
plan.rationale: This analysis is feasible using NHANES 2013-2018 data. Stroke is well-captured by self-report in MCQ160F. Sarcopenia requires derivation from DXA lean mass (DXXLEG + DXXARML) and grip strength (PAQ710) using established EWGSOP2 criteria. Key limitations: (1) Sarcopenia is not directly measured and must be derived; (2) DXA data availability may reduce sample size; (3) Gait speed may not be consistently available across cycles, limiting classification to probable/confirmed sarcopenia; (4) Self-reported stroke may have misclassification bias; (5) Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference. The 60+ age filter uses RIDAGEYR. Survey weights (WTMEC2YR) account for examination sampling and DXA subsample.
plan.guardrail_notes: DXA data may have missingness not at random - healthier individuals more likely to complete examination. Sarcopenia definition varies in literature (EWGSOP2 vs FNIH) - sensitivity analysis recommended. Stroke outcome is self-reported and may underestimate true prevalence, particularly for mild strokes. Sample size may be limited after requiring complete DXA, grip strength, and outcome data. Weight adjustment for merged cycles (divide by 3) assumes equal representativeness across cycles. Covariates like hypertension and diabetes are also self-reported. Consider interaction testing for sex differences in sarcopenia-stroke relationship.

guardrail_findings.message: weight 'WTMEC2YR' is not expressed as WTMEC2YR or WTSAF2YR divided by the number of cycles.
guardrail_findings.remediation: Expected rescaled weight expression: WTMEC2YR/3 (3 cycle(s) pooled).

guardrail_findings.message: Variable 'sarcopenia_status' (DXXLEG + DXXARML + PAQ710) is illustrative, not verified.
guardrail_findings.remediation: Acceptable for scaffolding; resolve before any reported result.

Observed associations between TBI and demographic factors reflect prevalence patterns, not causation.
plan.rationale: NHANES likely includes TBI or head injury-related questions in medical conditions or injury sections, though specific variable names may vary across cycles. Lifetime TBI may be captured under various question phrasings (concussion, head injury, brain injury, trauma). Recall bias and underreporting may affect prevalence estimates. Medical record verification is not available in NHANES. Recent cycles may have better TBI capture than earlier cycles. Consider potential under-ascertainment in older adults due to recall issues.

plan.question: Regional TBI incidence by age band (aggregate burden).

plan.title: Regional TBI prevalence by age band
plan.question: Regional TBI incidence by age band (aggregate burden)

outcome.name: tbi_prevalence
outcome.label: Traumatic brain injury prevalence
outcome.ref_notes: TBI data not available in NHANES

plan.rationale: Infeasible for multiple reasons: (1) NHANES is cross-sectional by design and cannot measure true disease 'incidence' (new cases over time); it can only assess prevalence (existing cases at time of survey). (2) TBI-specific data in NHANES is limited - there is no comprehensive TBI module. Limited head injury/concussion questions may exist in injury questionnaires but coverage is incomplete. (3) Geographic granularity is restricted due to NHANES privacy protections - data is typically limited to broad regions (Northeast, Midwest, South, West) with small sample sizes per region, preventing meaningful regional analysis. (4) 'Aggregate burden' is not directly measurable without utilization, cost, or disability metrics that NHANES does not provide in TBI-specific format.
plan.guardrail_notes: This question requires a different data source. Consider: (a) CDC's TBI surveillance system, (b) Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP) for incidence data, (c) Trauma Epidemiology databases, or (d) Vital Statistics mortality data for fatal TBI burden.

guardrail_findings.remediation: Confirm the codebook stem against the NHANES release before analysis.

plan.title: Feasibility assessment: tbi_03
plan.question: Post-concussion cognitive trajectories in older adults.

steps.description: Deterministic routing assessment: CHARLS matches an older-adult longitudinal cognitive question but its adapter is planned.

plan.rationale: CHARLS matches an older-adult longitudinal cognitive question but its adapter is planned.
plan.guardrail_notes: Deterministic router: CHARLS - CHARLS matches an older-adult longitudinal cognitive question but its adapter is planned.

plan.title: Feasibility assessment: Post-concussion cognitive trajectories in older adults
plan.question: Post-concussion cognitive trajectories in older adults

outcome.name: cognitive_score
outcome.label: Cognitive function score (not longitudinally available)
outcome.ref_notes: Cognitive testing available only at single time point per participant

plan.rationale: This research question is infeasible in NHANES because: (1) NHANES is primarily a cross-sectional survey design and does not include longitudinal cognitive assessments required to measure 'trajectories' or change over time within individuals; (2) While NHANES includes cognitive testing for older adults (ages 60+ or 70+ depending on cycle), these are administered only once per participant, preventing analysis of within-person change; (3) Concussion/head injury history, when available, is assessed at a single time point and does not provide the timing, severity, or recurrence data needed to establish temporal sequence for trajectory analysis; (4) The term 'trajectories' implies prospective longitudinal follow-up of cognitive function after concussion, which is beyond NHANES design capabilities. A cross-sectional association between remote head injury and current cognitive performance would be feasible, but this would not address the core question of post-concussion cognitive change over time.
plan.guardrail_notes: To study post-concussion cognitive trajectories, a longitudinal cohort study with repeated cognitive assessments would be required. NHANES is not designed for this type of analysis. Alternative cross-sectional analysis of head injury history and current cognitive performance would be feasible but would not address trajectories.

guardrail_findings.remediation: Confirm the codebook stem against the NHANES release before analysis.

outcome.name: na
outcome.label: Not applicable - NHANES does not capture glioblastoma survival data

plan.rationale: This question is not feasible for NHANES analysis. NHANES is a cross-sectional health survey designed to assess nutrition and health status in the US population, not a cancer registry. Glioblastoma is a rare brain tumor that would not be captured in sufficient numbers in NHANES. Additionally, NHANES does not collect detailed cancer registry data including tumor histology, extent of resection, or longitudinal survival outcomes required for this analysis. The appropriate data sources for this question would be dedicated cancer registries such as SEER (Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results) or NCDB (National Cancer Database), which are specifically designed to track cancer incidence, treatment patterns, and survival outcomes.
plan.guardrail_notes: Question requires cancer registry data (SEER, NCDB) rather than NHANES. Survival analysis of rare cancers needs dedicated oncology databases with longitudinal follow-up and detailed treatment variables.

plan.title: Feasibility assessment: tumor_02
plan.question: Is meningioma prevalence captured in NHANES?

steps.description: Deterministic routing assessment: NHANES has no brain-tumor histology capture

plan.rationale: NHANES has no brain-tumor histology capture
plan.guardrail_notes: Deterministic router: NHANES - NHANES has no brain-tumor histology capture

plan.title: Meningioma prevalence assessment in NHANES
plan.question: Is meningioma prevalence captured in NHANES?
plan.rationale: Meningioma is a rare intracranial tumor with population prevalence of approximately 97 cases per 100,000 persons. NHANES samples approximately 5,000 participants annually, meaning even across multiple 2-year cycles, the expected number of meningioma cases would be extremely low (likely <5-10 cases total). NHANES medical condition questionnaires (MCQ) capture general cancer diagnoses but lack specific histopathology detail to distinguish meningioma from other brain tumors. The survey design prioritizes common chronic conditions and nutrition-related outcomes; rare neoplasms with low prevalence are not suitable for cross-sectional analysis in this dataset.
plan.guardrail_notes: NHANES is not designed for rare disease epidemiology. Alternative data sources such as SEER (Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results) program, CBTRUS (Central Brain Tumor Registry in the United States), or hospital discharge databases would be more appropriate for meningioma prevalence research.

plan.title: Global brain/CNS cancer DALYs by region
plan.question: Global brain/CNS cancer disability-adjusted life-years by region
plan.rationale: This question is not feasible for NHANES analysis. NHANES is a US-specific cross-sectional health survey that does not collect global or regional data outside the United States. Additionally, NHANES does not contain disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) metrics, which are typically calculated from mortality and morbidity data found in disease registries or the Global Burden of Disease study. NHANES also lacks comprehensive cancer incidence, prevalence, or mortality data needed for DALY calculations. For this research question, alternative data sources such as the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study, WHO cancer mortality database, or cancer registries would be more appropriate.
plan.guardrail_notes: NHANES data is limited to the US population and does not include global health metrics or DALYs calculations. Consider using GBD study data or WHO databases for global cancer burden estimates.

plan.title: Fasting glucose, insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), and self-reported stroke association, NHANES 2015-2018
plan.question: Fasting glucose and insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) association with self-reported stroke, NHANES 2011-2014.

steps.description: Merge NHANES cycles G (2015-2016) and H (2017-2018) and apply inclusion criteria: adults with valid fasting glucose and insulin values
Calculate HOMA-IR = (fasting glucose [mg/dL] × insulin [µIU/mL]) / 405
Apply survey design with 4-year MEC weights appropriate for combined cycles
Log-transform HOMA-IR if skewed and examine associations with self-reported stroke using logistic regression
Adjust for age, sex, race/ethnicity, BMI, systolic blood pressure, and smoking status

causal_claims: Cross-sectional analysis likely identifies associations between fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, and self-reported stroke prevalence
Temporal direction uncertain given cross-sectional design—may suggest but cannot confirm metabolic dysregulation precedes stroke events
plan.rationale: NHANES is feasible for this analysis. Registry shows fasting glucose (LBXGLU) and insulin (LBXINS) available cycles G-J (2015-2022), while stroke self-report (MCQ160F) available C-J. Original request for 2011-2014 conflicts with lab data availability—analysis feasible using cycles G-H (2015-2018) where all required variables exist. HOMA-IR is calculated from fasting glucose and insulin. Uses fasting subsample (LAB variables require fasting). 4-year MEC weight accounts for combined cycles. Covariates include standard stroke risk factors.
plan.guardrail_notes: Original request specified 2011-2014 but lab measurements only available from 2015 onward—analysis restricted to cycles G-H (2015-2018). Self-reported stroke may underrepresent events, especially mild strokes or cognitive impairment preventing recall. Timing of stroke relative to metabolic measurements unknown—cross-sectional design limits causal inference. Fasting subsample reduces sample size and may introduce selection bias. HOMA-IR calculation assumes standard formula—may need log-transformation if skewed distribution. Combined cycle weighting requires careful application of 4-year MEC weights.

guardrail_findings.message: weight divisor 4 != number of cycles pooled (2)
guardrail_findings.remediation: Use WTSAF2YR/2.

guardrail_findings.message: Fasting subsample selected but weight base is not WTSAF2YR.
guardrail_findings.remediation: Fasting labs require WTSAF2YR, not WTMEC2YR.

plan.title: Association of fasting glucose and HOMA-IR with self-reported stroke in NHANES 2011-2014
plan.question: Fasting glucose and insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) association with self-reported stroke, NHANES 2011-2014.

steps.description: Merge NHANES cycles I (2011-2012) and J (2013-2014)
Identify participants with self-reported stroke (MCQ160E=1)
Restrict to fasting subsample participants with valid glucose and insulin measurements
Calculate HOMA-IR = (fasting_insulin[μU/mL] × fasting_glucose[mg/dL]) / 405
Apply NHANES survey design (SDMVPSU, SDMVSTRA, WTSAF2YR)
Descriptive statistics by stroke status
Multivariable logistic regression: stroke ~ fasting_glucose + covariates, accounting for complex survey design
Multivariable logistic regression: stroke ~ HOMA-IR + covariates, accounting for complex survey design
Sensitivity analyses: exclude participants with diabetes, stratify by BMI categories

causal_claims: Cross-sectional association may suggest relationship between dysglycemia/insulin resistance and stroke prevalence.
Directionality cannot be established due to cross-sectional design.
plan.rationale: Analysis is feasible with NHANES 2011-2014 data. Self-reported stroke is available from medical conditions questionnaire (MCQ). Fasting glucose and insulin are available from laboratory data for fasting subsample participants. HOMA-IR can be calculated from these measures. Appropriate survey weights (WTSAF2YR) account for fasting subsample design. Key limitation: cross-sectional design prevents causal inference - temporality between metabolic derangements and stroke cannot be established. Stroke is relatively rare in NHANES, which may limit statistical power.
plan.guardrail_notes: Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference. Self-reported stroke may be subject to recall bias and misclassification. Fasting subsample reduces sample size and statistical power. HOMA-IR calculation assumes fasting state (confirm using fasting variable). Stroke prevalence may be too low for stable estimates; consider collapsing with other cerebrovascular outcomes or using broader timeframe. Residual confounding likely despite comprehensive covariate adjustment.

guardrail_findings.message: weight divisor 4 != number of cycles pooled (2)
guardrail_findings.remediation: Use WTSAF2YR/2.
